theorems.title: Uniform convergence plus empirical optimization implies learning
theorems.section: 2.5 Relation between learning and optimization
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \( u>0\) and \(0<\alpha,\delta<1\). Suppose the sample size \(m=m(\alpha, u,\delta)\) is such that for all probability distributions \(P\in\mathcal P\)

\[\Pr igl(\exists h\in H:d_ u(\widehat r_h(\vec z),r_h(P))>\alpha/3 igr)\leq\delta/2,\]

where the \(z_i\in\vec z\), \(1\leq i\leq m\), are drawn independently at random from \(Z\) according to the distribution \(P\). Suppose also that the algorithm \(A\) is such that for all \(P\in\mathcal P\)

\[\Pr igl(d_ u(\widehat r_{A(\vec z)}(\vec z),\widehat r^*(\vec z))>\alpha/3 igr)\leq\delta/2,\]

where \(\vec z\) is drawn randomly by \(P\) as above. Then for all \(P\in\mathcal P\)

\[\Pr igl(d_ u(r_{A(\vec z)}(P),r^*(P))>\alpha igr)\leq\delta,\]

i.e. \(A\) solves the basic learning problem for the family of \(L_{\alpha, u}\) regret functions and has sample complexity at most \(m(\alpha, u,\delta)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
By the triangle inequality for \(d_ u\), if

  1. \(d_ u(r_{A(\vec z)}(P),\widehat r_{A(\vec z)}(\vec z))\leq\alpha/3\),
  2. \(d_ u(\widehat r_{A(\vec z)}(\vec z),\widehat r^*(\vec z))\leq\alpha/3\), and
  3. \(d_ u(\widehat r^*(\vec z),r^*(P))\leq\alpha/3\),

then \[d_ u(r_{A(\vec z)}(P),r^*(P))\leq\alpha.\]

The second assumption of the lemma states that (2) holds with probability at least \(1-\delta/2\). The first assumption implies that both (1) and (3) hold with probability at least \(1-\delta/2\). (If (3) fails then we can find a decision rule \(h\in H\) such that \(d_ u(\widehat r_h(\vec z),r_h(P))>\alpha/3\). Here we use the compatibility of \(d_ u\) with the ordering on the reals.) Hence with probability at least \(1-\delta\) all of (1)–(3) hold. The result follows.

theorems.title: Uniform estimates for a finite function class
theorems.section: 3.1 The case of finite F
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be a finite set of functions on \(Z\) with \(0\leq f(z)\leq M\) for all \(f\in F\) and \(z\in Z\). Let \(\vec z=(z_1,\ldots,z_m)\) be a sequence of \(m\) examples drawn independently from \(Z\) according to any distribution on \(Z\), and let \(\epsilon>0\). Then

\[\Pr igl(\exists f\in F:|\widehat E_{\vec z}(f)-E(f)|>\epsilon igr)\leq 2|F|e^{-2\epsilon^2m/M^2}.\]

For \(0<\delta\leq1\) and sample size

\[m\geq rac{M^2}{2\epsilon^2}\left(\ln|F|+\ln rac2\delta ight)\]

this probability is at most \(\delta\). Further, for any \( u>0\) and \(0<\alpha<1\),

\[\Pr igl(\exists f\in F:d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha igr)\leq 2|F|e^{-\alpha^2 u m/M}.\]

For \(0<\delta\leq1\) and sample size

\[m\geq rac{M}{\alpha^2 u}\left(\ln|F|+\ln rac2\delta ight)\]

this probability is at most \(\delta\).
theorems.proofHtml:
For the second part of the theorem, using Bernstein's inequality (see e.g. [Pol84]) it is easy to show that for any single function \(f\) with \(0\leq f\leq M\),

\[\Pr igl(d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha igr)<2e^{-\alpha^2 u m/M}.\]

Details are given in Lemma 9, part (2) in the Appendix. It follows that the probability that there is any \(f\in F\) with \(d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha\) is at most \(2|F|e^{-\alpha^2 u m/M}\). Setting this bound to \(\delta\) and solving for \(m\) gives the result on the sample size. The proof of the first part of the lemma is similar, except we use Hoeffding's inequality (see e.g. [Pol84]), which implies that for any single \(f\),

\[\Pr igl(|\widehat E_{\vec z}(f)-E(f)|>\epsilon igr)\leq2e^{-2\epsilon^2m/M^2}.\]

theorems.title: Uniform relative-deviation bound by random covering numbers
theorems.section: 3.2 The general case
theorems.proofNote: The paper states that the proof is in Appendix 10.4. It proves the stronger Theorem 3 there and then obtains Theorem 2 as the displayed corollary on PDF page 28.
theorems.statementHtml:
([Pol86]) Let \(F\) be a permissible set of functions on \(Z\) with \(0\leq f(z)\leq M\) for all \(f\in F\) and \(z\in Z\). Let \(\vec z=(z_1,\ldots,z_m)\) be a sequence of \(m\) examples drawn independently from \(Z\) according to any distribution on \(Z\). Then for any \( u>0\) and \(0<\alpha<1\),

\[\Pr igl(\exists f\in F:d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha igr)\leq4E\!\left(\mathcal N(\alpha u/8,F|_{\vec z}) ight)e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(16M)}.\]
theorems.proofHtml: Theorem 2 is obtained as a corollary of this result by substituting \(m/2\) for \(m\) and not taking the minimum with 1 in the left hand side of the first bound for \(p(\alpha,\nu,m)\).

theorems.title: Uniform absolute-deviation bound by random covering numbers
theorems.section: 3.2 The general case
theorems.statementHtml:
([Pol84]) Under the same assumptions as above, for all \(\epsilon>0\),

\[\Pr\bigl(\exists f\in F:|\widehat E_{\vec z}(f)-E(f)|>\epsilon\bigr)\leq4E\!\left(\mathcal N(\epsilon/16,F|_{\vec z})\right)e^{-\epsilon^2m/(128M^2)}.\]
theorems.proofHtml: This follows directly from the above result by setting \( u=2M\), and \(\alpha=\epsilon/(4M)\). To see this, note that property (3) of the \(d_ u\) metric (section 2.2) implies that \(|r-s|\leq\epsilon\) whenever \(d_ u(r,s)\leq\alpha\) for all \(0\leq r,s\leq M\) when this setting of \( u\) and \(\alpha\) is used.

theorems.title: Truncated random-cover uniform convergence bound
theorems.section: 3.2 The general case
theorems.proofNote: Appendix 10.4 proves this theorem through Lemmas 10–14. Lemma 13 gives the corresponding bound in the extended metric and Lemma 14 relates that metric to the L1 metric.
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be a permissible set of functions on \(Z\) with \(0\leq f(z)\leq M\) for all \(f\in F\) and \(z\in Z\). Assume \( u>0\), \(0<\alpha<1\) and \(m\geq1\). Suppose that \(\vec z\) is generated by \(m\) independent random draws according to any probability measure on \(Z\). Let

\[p(\alpha, u,m)=\Pr\{\vec z\in Z^m:\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha\}.\]

Then

\[p(\alpha, u,m)\leq2E\!\left(\min\left(2\mathcal N(\alpha u/8,F|_{\vec z};d_{L_1})e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)},1 ight) ight),\]

where the expectation is over \(\vec z\) drawn randomly from \(Z^{2m}\). If in addition \(F|_{\vec z}\) is finite for all \(\vec z\in Z^{2m}\) then

\[p(\alpha, u,m)\leq2E\!\left(\min\left(2|F|_{\vec z}|e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(2M)},1 ight) ight).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
In this section we prove Theorem 3 from section 3. The proof is given in a series of lemmas. We first extend the metric \(d_\nu\) to a pseudo-metric on vectors in \((\mathbb R^+)^{2m}\) for \(m\geq1\). We will do so in a somewhat unusual manner that will be useful in what follows. In fact, this extension can be defined for any metric, so we will state it in its general form.

The theorem follows easily from the last two lemmas.

theorems.title: A hyperplane misses an orthant
theorems.section: 4 Pseudo dimension of classes of real-valued functions
theorems.statementHtml: No hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^d\) intersects all orthants of \(\mathbb R^d\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(T\) be a hyperplane in \(\mathbb R^d\). Choose a vector \(\vec x\in\mathbb R^d\) as follows. If \(T\) includes the origin, then let \(\vec x\) be any vector that is orthogonal to \(T\) and has at least one strictly negative coordinate. (For any nonzero orthogonal vector \(\vec x\), if \(\vec x\) doesn't have a negative coordinate then \(-\vec x\) does.) Otherwise let \(\vec x\) be the (nonzero) vector in \(T\) on the line perpendicular to \(T\) that passes through the origin. To complete the proof, we show that for all \(\vec y\in T\), \(\operatorname{sign}(\vec y)\neq\vec 1-\operatorname{sign}(\vec x)\), where \(\vec1\) denotes the all 1's vector.

Suppose to the contrary that \(\operatorname{sign}(\vec y)=\vec1-\operatorname{sign}(\vec x)\) for some \(\vec y\in T\). This implies that the inner product \(\sum_{i=1}^d x_i y_i\) is non-positive, and is in fact strictly negative if either \(\vec x\) or \(\vec y\) contain a strictly negative coordinate. However, by our choice of \(\vec x\), either \(\vec x\) is orthogonal to \(\vec y\) and contains a strictly negative coordinate, giving an immediate contradiction, or \(\vec x\) is non-zero and \(\vec x\) is orthogonal to \(\vec y-\vec x\). In this last case,

\[\sum_{i=1}^d x_i y_i=\sum_{i=1}^d x_i^2,\]

which is again a contradiction, since the left side is non-positive while the right side is strictly positive.

theorems.title: Pseudo dimension of a vector space
theorems.section: 4 Pseudo dimension of classes of real-valued functions
theorems.statementHtml: (Dudley) Let \(F\) be a \(d\)-dimensional vector space of functions from a set \(Z\) into \(\mathbb R\). Then \(\dim_P(F)=d\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Fix any sequence \(\vec z=(z_1,\ldots,z_{d+1})\) of points in \(Z\). For any \(f\in F\) let \(\Psi(f)=(f(z_1),\ldots,f(z_{d+1}))\). Then \(\Psi\) is a linear mapping from \(F\) into \(\mathbb R^{d+1}\), and the image of \(\Psi\) is \(F|_{\vec z}\). Since \(F\) is a vector space of dimension \(d\), this implies that \(F|_{\vec z}\) is a subspace of \(\mathbb R^{d+1}\) of dimension at most \(d\). Hence by Lemma 2, \(F|_{\vec z}\) is not full. This implies \(\dim_P(F)\leq d\).

On the other hand, if \(F\) is a \(d\)-dimensional vector space of real-valued functions on \(Z\), then there exists a sequence \(\vec z\) of \(d\) points in \(Z\) such that \(F|_{\vec z}=\mathbb R^d\). Hence \(\vec z\) is shattered, implying that \(\dim_P(F)\geq d\).

theorems.title: Pseudo-dimension invariance properties
theorems.section: 4 Pseudo dimension of classes of real-valued functions
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be a family of functions from \(Z\) into \(\mathbb R\). Fix any function \(g\) from \(Z\) into \(\mathbb R\) and let \(G=\{g+f:f\in F\}\). Let \(I\) be a real interval (possibly all of \(\mathbb R\)) such that every function in \(F\) takes values only in \(I\). Fix any nondecreasing (resp. nonincreasing) function \(h:I\to\mathbb R\) and let \(H=\{h\circ f:f\in F\}\). Then

  1. ([WD81]) \(\dim_P(G)=\dim_P(F)\), and
  2. ([NP87,Dud87]) \(\dim_P(H)\leq\dim_P(F)\), with equality if \(h\) is continuous and strictly increasing (resp. continuous and strictly decreasing).
theorems.proofHtml:
Part (1) follows directly from the fact that the notion of a set of points being full is invariant under translation. For part (2) it suffices to prove the results for \(h\) nondecreasing and \(h\) continuous and strictly increasing. Let \(\vec z=(z_1,\ldots,z_d)\) be such that \(H|_{\vec z}\) is full, i.e. such that \(H|_{\vec z}-\vec x\) intersects all \(2^d\) orthants of \(\mathbb R^d\) for some vector \(\vec x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d)\) in \(\mathbb R^d\). Then for every Boolean vector \(\vec b\in\{0,1\}^d\) there exists a function \(f_{\vec b}\in F\) such that for every \(i\), \(1\leq i\leq d\), we have \(h\circ f_{\vec b}(z_i)>x_i\) if and only if the \(i\)th bit of \(\vec b\) is 1. For each \(i\), let

\[u_i=\min\{f_{\vec b}(z_i):\text{the }i\text{th bit of }\vec b\text{ is }1\}\]

and

\[l_i=\max\{f_{\vec b}(z_i):\text{the }i\text{th bit of }\vec b\text{ is }0\}.\]

Since \(h\) is nondecreasing, we have \(u_i>l_i\) for each \(i\). Let \(r_i=(u_i+l_i)/2\) for each \(i\) and \(\vec r=(r_1,\ldots,r_d)\). Let \(T=\{f_{\vec b}:\vec b\in\{0,1\}^d\}\). Then clearly \(T-\vec r\) intersects every orthant of \(\mathbb R^d\), so \(T\) is full. Since \(T\subseteq F\), this implies that \(F|_{\vec z}\) is full, and hence \(\dim_P(H)\leq\dim_P(F)\). Equality follows when \(h\) is continuous and strictly increasing since we obtain the class \(F\) from \(H\) by composing with \(h^{-1}\).

theorems.title: Packing bound from pseudo dimension
theorems.section: 4 Pseudo dimension of classes of real-valued functions
theorems.statementHtml:
(Pollard) Let \(F\) be a family of functions from a set \(Z\) into \([0,M]\), where \(\dim_P(F)=d\) for some \(1\leq d<\infty\). Let \(P\) be a probability measure on \(Z\). Then for all \(0<\epsilon\leq M\),

\[\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})<2\left(\frac{2eM}{\epsilon}\ln\frac{2eM}{\epsilon}\right)^d.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Since \(\dim_P(F)=d\), it follows from Sauer's lemma that \(|\operatorname{sign}(F|_{\vec z}-\vec r)|\leq(em/d)^d\) for all \(m\geq d\), \(\vec z\in Z^m\) and \(\vec r\in[0,M]^m\). Hence the above lemma implies that

\[(em/d)^d\geq\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})\left(1-\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})e^{-\epsilon m/M}\right)\tag{4}\]

for all probability measures \(P\) on \(Z\) and \(m\geq d\). It is easily verified that if

\[\frac M\epsilon\ln\bigl(2\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})\bigr)<d\]

then the upper bound given in Theorem 6 follows trivially using the fact that \(\epsilon\leq M\). Thus we may assume that \(\frac M\epsilon\ln(2\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)}))\geq d\). Hence, if \(m\geq\frac M\epsilon\ln(2\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)}))\), then \(m\geq d\) and

\[1-\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})e^{-\epsilon m/M}\geq1/2.\]

Thus from (4) we obtain

\[\left(\frac{eM\ln(2\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)}))}{\epsilon d}\right)^d\geq\frac12\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)}).\]

With some simple calculations, this gives the bound of Theorem 6.

theorems.title: Sauer's lemma
theorems.section: 4 Pseudo dimension of classes of real-valued functions
theorems.proofNote: The paper says it gives this standard result without proof.
theorems.statementHtml:
(Sauer) Let \(F\) be a class of functions from \(S=\{1,2,\ldots,m\}\) into \(\{0,1\}\) with \(|F|>1\) and let \(d\) be the length of the longest sequence of points \(\vec z\) from \(S\) such that \(F|_{\vec z}=\{0,1\}^d\). Then

\[|F|\leq\sum_{i=0}^d\binom mi\leq(em/d)^d,\]

where \(e\) is the base of the natural logarithm.

theorems.title: Random orthants lower-bound packing
theorems.section: 4 Pseudo dimension of classes of real-valued functions
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be a family of functions from a set \(Z\) into \([0,M]\) and let \(P\) be a probability measure on \(Z\). Let \(\vec r=(r_1,\ldots,r_m)\) be a random vector in \([0,M]^m\) where each \(r_i\) is drawn independently at random from the uniform distribution on \([0,M]\). Let \(\vec z=(z_1,\ldots,z_m)\) be a random vector in \(Z^m\) where each \(z_i\) is drawn independently at random from \(P\). Then for all \(\epsilon>0\)

\[E\bigl(|\operatorname{sign}(F|_{\vec z}-\vec r)|\bigr)\geq\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})\left(1-\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})e^{-\epsilon m/M}\right).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
For all \(f\in F\) we will denote \((f(z_1),\ldots,f(z_m))\) by \(f|_{\vec z}\). Choose \(\epsilon>0\). Let \(G\) be an \(\epsilon\)-separated subset of \(F\) (w.r.t. \(d_{L_1(P)}\)), with \(|G|=\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})\). Then

\[ egin{aligned}E(|\operatorname{sign}(F|_{\vec z}-\vec r)|)&\geq E(|\operatorname{sign}(G|_{\vec z}-\vec r)|)\&\geq E(|\{f\in G:\operatorname{sign}(f|_{\vec z}-\vec r) eq\operatorname{sign}(g|_{\vec z}-\vec r) ext{ for all }g\in G,g eq f\}|)\&=\sum_{f\in G}\Pr(\operatorname{sign}(f|_{\vec z}-\vec r) eq\operatorname{sign}(g|_{\vec z}-\vec r) ext{ for all }g\in G,g eq f)\&=\sum_{f\in G}\left(1-\Pr(\exists g\in G,g eq f:\operatorname{sign}(f|_{\vec z}-\vec r)=\operatorname{sign}(g|_{\vec z}-\vec r)) ight)\&\geq\sum_{f\in G}\left(1-|G|\max_{g\in G,g eq f}\Pr(\operatorname{sign}(f|_{\vec z}-\vec r)=\operatorname{sign}(g|_{\vec z}-\vec r)) ight).\end{aligned}\]

Let \(f\) and \(g\) be distinct functions in \(G\). Since \(G\) is \(\epsilon\)-separated,

\[\int_Z|f(z)-g(z)|\,dP(z)>\epsilon.\]

In addition, the range of \(f\) and \(g\) is \([0,M]\). Hence if \(z_i\) is drawn at random from \(P\) and \(r_i\) drawn at random from the uniform distribution on \([0,M]\), then the probability that \(r_i\) lies between \(f(z_i)\) and \(g(z_i)\) is at least \(\epsilon/M\). And \(\operatorname{sign}(f|_{\vec z}-\vec r)=\operatorname{sign}(g|_{\vec z}-\vec r)\) only if this fails to occur for each \(i\), \(1\leq i\leq m\). Thus

\[\Pr(\operatorname{sign}(f|_{\vec z}-\vec r)=\operatorname{sign}(g|_{\vec z}-\vec r))\leq(1-\epsilon/M)^m\leq e^{-\epsilon m/M}.\]

Since this holds for every distinct \(f\) and \(g\) in \(G\), combining this with the inequality above, we have

\[E(|\operatorname{sign}(F|_{\vec z}-\vec r)|)\geq|G|(1-|G|e^{-\epsilon m/M}).\]

Since \(|G|=\mathcal M(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P)})\), this gives the result.

theorems.title: Uniform convergence from finite pseudo dimension
theorems.section: 4 Pseudo dimension of classes of real-valued functions
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be a permissible family of functions from a set \(Z\) into \([0,M]\) with \(\dim_P(F)=d\) for some \(1\leq d<\infty\). Assume \(m\geq1\), \(0< u\leq8M\) and \(0<\alpha<1\). Let \(\vec z\) be generated by \(m\) independent draws according to any distribution on \(Z\). Then

\[\Pr igl(\exists f\in F:d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha igr)\leq8\left( rac{16eM}{\alpha u}\ln rac{16eM}{\alpha u} ight)^d e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}.\]

Moreover, for

\[m\geq rac{8M}{\alpha^2 u}\left(2d\ln rac{8eM}{\alpha u}+\ln rac8\delta ight)\]

this probability is at most \(\delta\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(\epsilon=\alpha u/8\). Since \(\alpha<1\) and \( u\leq8M\), \(\epsilon\leq M\). For any sequence \(\vec z\) of points in \(Z\) there is a trivial isometry between \((F|_{\vec z},d_{L_1})\) and \((F,d_{L_1(P_{\vec z})})\), where \(P_{\vec z}\) is the empirical measure induced by \(\vec z\), in which each set has measure equal to the fraction of the points in \(\vec z\) it contains. Thus by Theorem 12 of section 10.1 and Theorem 6, we have

\[\mathcal N(\epsilon,F|_{\vec z};d_{L_1})\leq\mathcal M(\epsilon,F|_{\vec z};d_{L_1})\leq2\left( rac{2eM}{\epsilon}\ln rac{2eM}{\epsilon} ight)^d\]

for all \(\vec z\in Z^{2m}\). Hence the given probability is at most

\[8\left( rac{2eM}{\epsilon}\ln rac{2eM}{\epsilon} ight)^d e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}=8\left( rac{16eM}{\alpha u}\ln rac{16eM}{\alpha u} ight)^d e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}\]

by Theorem 3.

For the second result, setting the bound above equal to \(\delta\) and solving for \(m\) gives

\[m\geq rac{8M}{\alpha^2 u}\left(d\ln\left( rac{16eM}{\alpha u}\ln rac{16eM}{\alpha u} ight)+\ln rac8\delta ight).\]

It is easily verified that \(\ln(a\ln a)<2\ln(a/2)\) when \(a\geq5\), and from this the bound given in the second result follows.

theorems.title: Absolute uniform convergence from pseudo dimension
theorems.section: 4 Pseudo dimension of classes of real-valued functions
theorems.statementHtml:
Under the same assumptions as above, for all \(0<\epsilon\leq M\),

\[\Pr\bigl(\exists f\in F:|\widehat E_{\vec z}(f)-E(f)|>\epsilon\bigr)\leq8\left(\frac{32eM}{\epsilon}\ln\frac{32eM}{\epsilon}\right)^d e^{-\epsilon^2m/(64M^2)}.\]

Moreover, for

\[m\geq\frac{64M^2}{\epsilon^2}\left(2d\ln\frac{16eM}{\epsilon}+\ln\frac8\delta\right)\]

this probability is at most \(\delta\).
theorems.proofHtml: This follows directly from the above result by setting \(\nu=2M\), \(\alpha=\epsilon/(4M)\), and using property (3) of the \(d_\nu\) metric, as in the proof of Corollary 1 in section 3.2.

theorems.title: Monotone losses preserve pseudo dimension
theorems.section: 5 Some Applications of Pseudo Dimension in Learning
theorems.statementHtml: If \(A\subseteq\mathbb R\) and \(l\) is a loss function on \(A\times Y\) that is monotone over \(A\), then \(\dim_P(l_H)=\dim_P(H)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Suppose that \(\vec x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d)\) is shattered by \(H\), where \(x_i\in X\), \(1\leq i\leq d\). Then there is some real vector \(\vec r=(r_1,\ldots,r_d)\) such that \(H|_{\vec x}-\vec r\) intersects all \(2^d\) orthants of \(\mathbb R^d\). Hence for every Boolean vector \(\vec b\in\{0,1\}^d\) there exists a function \(h_{\vec b}\in H\) such that for every \(i\), \(h_{\vec b}(x_i)>r_i\) if and only if the \(i\)th bit of \(\vec b\) is 1. Fix an outcome \(y\in Y\). Let \(\vec z=(z_1,\ldots,z_d)\), where \(z_i=(x_i,y)\) for all \(i\). Note that if \(f_y\) is strictly increasing, then for any \(h\in H\) and \(1\leq i\leq d\), \(h(x_i)>r_i\iff l_h(z_i)=l(y,h(x_i))=f_y(h(x_i))>f_y(r_i)\). Hence, for every Boolean vector \(\vec b\in\{0,1\}^d\) there exists a function \(h_{\vec b}\in H\) such that for every \(i\), we have \(l_{h_{\vec b}}(z_i)>f_y(r_i)\) if and only if the \(i\)th bit of \(\vec b\) is 1. A similar result holds if \(f_y\) is strictly decreasing. Thus \(\vec z\) is shattered by \(l_H\). It follows that \(\dim_P(l_H)\geq\dim_P(H)\).

For the other direction, assume \(\vec z=(z_1,\ldots,z_d)\) is shattered by \(l_H\), where \(z_i=(x_i,y_i)\). We will show that \(\vec x=(x_1,\ldots,x_d)\) is shattered by \(H\). Since \(\vec z\) is shattered by \(l_H\), there is some real vector \(\vec r=(r_1,\ldots,r_d)\) such that for every Boolean vector \(\vec b\in\{0,1\}^d\) there exists a function \(h_{\vec b}\in H\) such that for every \(i\), \(l_{h_{\vec b}}(z_i)>r_i\) if and only if the \(i\)th bit of \(\vec b\) is 1. Let \(A_0=\{h_{\vec b}(x_i):1\leq i\leq d\text{ and }\vec b\in\{0,1\}^d\}\). For each outcome \(y\in Y\) define the function \(g_y:\mathbb R\to A_0\) as follows. If \(f_y\) is increasing then \(g_y(r)=\max\{a\in A_0:f_y(a)\leq r\}\) and if \(f_y\) is decreasing then \(g_y(r)=\max\{a\in A_0:f_y(a)>r\}\). Then for each \(i\), either

  1. for all \(h\in H\), \(l_h(z_i)=f_{y_i}(h(x_i))>r_i\iff h(x_i)>g_{y_i}(r_i)\), or
  2. for all \(h\in H\), \(l_h(z_i)=f_{y_i}(h(x_i))>r_i\iff h(x_i)\leq g_{y_i}(r_i)\).

Hence, for every Boolean vector \(\vec b\in\{0,1\}^d\) there exists a function \(h'_{\vec b}\in H\) such that for every \(i\), \(h'_{\vec b}(x_i)>g_{y_i}(r_i)\) if and only if the \(i\)th bit of \(\vec b\) is 1. (To see this, let \(\vec c\) be the Boolean vector derived from \(\vec b\) by complementing the bit in each position \(i\) for which \(f_{y_i}\) is decreasing, and then let \(h'_{\vec b}=h_{\vec c}\).) Thus \(\vec x\) is shattered by \(H\). It follows that \(\dim_P(H)\geq\dim_P(l_H)\), and combined with the above inequality, this gives the result.

theorems.title: Uniform convergence for monotone real-valued loss
theorems.section: 5 Some Applications of Pseudo Dimension in Learning
theorems.statementHtml:
Assume the decision space \(A\subseteq\mathbb R\), the loss function \(l\) is monotone over \(A\) and bounded between 0 and \(M\), the decision rule space \(H\) is such that \(l_H\) is permissible, and \(1\leq d=\dim_P(H)<\infty\). Assume \(m\geq1\), \(0< u<8M\) and \(0<\alpha<1\). Let \(P\) be any probability distribution on \(Z\). Let \(\vec z\) be generated by \(m\) independent draws from \(Z\) according to \(P\). Then

\[\Pr igl(\exists h\in H:d_ u(\widehat r_{h;l}(\vec z),r_{h;l}(P))>\alpha igr)\leq8\left( rac{16eM}{\alpha u}\ln rac{16eM}{\alpha u} ight)^d e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}.\]

Moreover, for

\[m\geq rac{8M}{\alpha^2 u}\left(2d\ln rac{8eM}{\alpha u}+\ln rac8\delta ight)\]

this probability is at most \(\delta\).
theorems.proofHtml: By Lemma 5, \(\dim_P(l_H)=\dim_P(H)\) in this case. Thus, since \(\widehat r_{h;l}(\vec z)=\widehat E_{\vec z}(l_h)\) and \(r_{h;l}(P)=E(l_h)\), the result follows directly from Theorem 7.

theorems.title: Loss-class covering contraction
theorems.section: 6 Capacity and Metric Dimension of Function Classes
theorems.statementHtml:
For all \(\epsilon>0\),

\[\mathcal N(\epsilon,l_H,d_{L_1(P)})\leq\mathcal N(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1(P|_X;\rho_l)}).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
For every \(h\in H\) let \(\psi(h)=l_h\). Hence \(\psi\) maps from \(H\) onto \(l_H\). It suffices to show that \(\psi\) is a contraction, i.e. that

\[\forall f,g\in H,\quad d_{L_1(P)}(\psi(f),\psi(g))\leq d_{L_1(P|_X;\rho_l)}(f,g).\]

Let \(f\) and \(g\) be any two functions in \(H\). Then

\[\begin{aligned}d_{L_1(P)}(\psi(f),\psi(g))&=\int_Z|l(y,f(x))-l(y,g(x))|\,dP(x,y)\\&\leq\int_Z\rho_l(f(x),g(x))\,dP(x,y)\\&=\int_X\rho_l(f(x),g(x))\,dP|_X(x)\\&=d_{L_1(P|_X;\rho_l)}(f,g).\end{aligned}\]

theorems.title: Uniform convergence from capacity
theorems.section: 6 Capacity and Metric Dimension of Function Classes
theorems.statementHtml:
Assume that the decision rule space \(H\) and the loss function \(l\) are such that \(l_H\) is permissible. Let \(P\) be any probability distribution on \(Z=X imes Y\). Assume \(m\geq1\), \( u>0\) and \(0<\alpha<1\). Let \(\vec z\) be generated by \(m\) independent draws from \(Z\) according to \(P\). Then

\[\Pr igl(\exists h\in H:d_ u(\widehat r_{h;l}(\vec z),r_{h;l}(P))>\alpha igr)\leq4\mathcal C(\alpha u/8,H, ho_l)e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(F=l_H\). For any sequence \(\vec z\) of points in \(Z\) there is a trivial isometry between \((F|_{\vec z},d_{L_1})\) and \((F,d_{L_1(P_{\vec z})})\), where \(P_{\vec z}\) is the empirical measure induced by \(\vec z\), in which each set has measure equal to the fraction of the points in \(\vec z\) it contains. Thus by Lemma 6 above, we have

\[\mathcal N(\epsilon,F|_{\vec z},d_{L_1})=\mathcal N(\epsilon,F,d_{L_1(P_{\vec z})})\leq\mathcal N(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1(P_{\vec z}|_X; ho_l)})\leq\mathcal C(\epsilon,H, ho_l)\]

for all \(\vec z\in Z^{2m}\). Hence, setting \(\epsilon=\alpha u/8\), the given probability is at most \(4\mathcal C(\alpha u/8,H, ho_l)e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}\) by Theorem 3.

theorems.title: Capacity of a free product
theorems.section: 6 Capacity and Metric Dimension of Function Classes
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(H,H_1,\ldots,H_k\) are defined as above then

  1. for any probability measure \(P\) on \(X\) and \(\epsilon>0\),

    \[\prod_{j=1}^k\mathcal N(2k\epsilon,H_j,d_{L_1(P;\rho_j)})\leq\mathcal N(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1(P;\rho)})\leq\prod_{j=1}^k\mathcal N(\epsilon,H_j,d_{L_1(P;\rho_j)});\]

  2. \(\overline{\dim}(H)=\sum_{j=1}^k\overline{\dim}(H_j)\), and similarly for \(\underline{\dim}\) and \(\dim\), when the latter is defined.
theorems.proofHtml:
We begin with the second inequality of (1). For each \(1\leq j\leq k\) let \(U_j\) be an \(\epsilon\)-cover for \(H_j\). Let

\[U=\prod_{j=1}^kU_j=\{(f_1,\ldots,f_k):f_j\in U_j,1\leq j\leq k\}.\]

It suffices to show that \(U\) is an \(\epsilon\)-cover for \(H\). Let \(g=(g_1,\ldots,g_k)\) be any function in \(H\). For each \(j\), find \(f_j\in U_j\) such that \(d_{L_1(P;\rho_j)}(f_j,g_j)\leq\epsilon\). Let \(f=(f_1,\ldots,f_k)\). Then

\[\begin{aligned}d_{L_1(P;\rho)}(f,g)&=\int_X\frac1k\sum_{j=1}^k\rho_j(f_j(x),g_j(x))\,dP(x)\\&=\frac1k\sum_{j=1}^k\int_X\rho_j(f_j(x),g_j(x))\,dP(x)\\&=\frac1k\sum_{j=1}^kd_{L_1(P;\rho_j)}(f_j,g_j)\leq\epsilon.\end{aligned}\]

Hence \(U\) is an \(\epsilon\)-cover for \(H\).

The first inequality of (1) is verified similarly. For each \(1\leq j\leq k\) let \(V_j\) be a \(k\epsilon\)-separated subset of \(H_j\). Let \(V=\prod_{j=1}^kV_j\). Let \(f=(f_1,\ldots,f_k)\) and \(g=(g_1,\ldots,g_k)\) be distinct functions in \(V\). Then

\[d_{L_1(P;\rho)}(f,g)=\frac1k\sum_{j=1}^kd_{L_1(P;\rho_j)}(f_j,g_j)>\epsilon.\]

Hence \(V\) is an \(\epsilon\)-separated subset of \(H\). It follows that \(\prod_{j=1}^k\mathcal M(k\epsilon,H_j,d_{L_1(P;\rho_j)})\leq\mathcal M(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1(P;\rho)})\). The first inequality of (1) then follows using Theorem 12.

From (1) we have

\[\prod_{j=1}^k\mathcal C(2k\epsilon,H_j,\rho_j)\leq\mathcal C(\epsilon,H,\rho)\leq\prod_{j=1}^k\mathcal C(\epsilon,H_j,\rho_j).\]

Part (2) follows easily from this.

theorems.title: Capacity of composed Lipschitz classes
theorems.section: 6 Capacity and Metric Dimension of Function Classes
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \((X_1,\rho_1),\ldots,(X_{k+1},\rho_{k+1})\) be metric spaces, where \((X_j,\rho_j)\) is bounded, \(2\leq j\leq k\), and \(H_j\) be a class of functions with \(f:X_j\to X_{j+1}\) for all \(f\in H_j\), \(1\leq j\leq k\). Let \(b_j\) be a uniform Lipschitz bound on \(H_j\) for all \(2\leq j\leq k\). Let \(H\) denote the class of all functions from \(X_1\) into \(X_{k+1}\) defined by compositions of functions in the \(H_j\)'s, i.e.

\[H=\{f_k\circ f_{k-1}\circ\cdots\circ f_1:f_j\in H_j,1\leq j\leq k\}.\]

  1. For any \(\epsilon,\epsilon_1,\ldots,\epsilon_k>0\) such that

    \[\epsilon=\sum_{j=1}^k\left(\prod_{l=j+1}^kb_l\right)\epsilon_j\]

    we have

    \[\mathcal C(\epsilon,H,\rho_{k+1})\leq\prod_{j=1}^k\mathcal C(\epsilon_j,H_j,\rho_{j+1}).\]

  2. \(\overline{\dim}(H)\leq\sum_{j=1}^k\overline{\dim}(H_j)\), and similarly for \(\underline{\dim}\) and \(\dim\), when the latter is defined.
theorems.proofHtml:
Fix a probability measure \(P\) on \(X_1\). We define a tree-structured family of covers for the \(H_j\)'s by induction as follows. For the basis case, let \(U\) be a minimum-sized \(\epsilon_1\)-cover for \(H_1\) w.r.t. the measure \(P\) on \(X_1\), i.e. \(|U|=\mathcal N(\epsilon_1,H_1,d_{L_1(P;\rho_2)})\) and every function in \(H_1\) is \(L_1(P;\rho_2)\)-approximated to within \(\epsilon_1\) by some function in \(U\). Now for each \(j\), \(2\leq j\leq k\), and for each sequence of functions \(f_1,\ldots,f_{j-1}\) selected from the preceding covers, let \(U_{f_1,\ldots,f_{j-1}}\) be a minimum-sized \(\epsilon_j\)-cover for \(H_j\) w.r.t. the \(L_1\) metric for the measure \(P_{f_{j-1}\circ\cdots\circ f_1}\) on \(X_j\) and the metric \(\rho_{j+1}\) on \(X_{j+1}\).

Next we define a cover \(V\) for \(H\) by composing functions in the covers for the \(H_j\)'s. If \(k=1\) then \(V=U\). Otherwise \(V\) consists of all \(f_k\circ\cdots\circ f_1\) selected along these cover trees. Since \(\mathcal N(\epsilon_j,H_j,d_{L_1(P_{f_{j-1}\circ\cdots\circ f_1};\rho_{j+1})})\leq\mathcal C(\epsilon_j,H_j,\rho_{j+1})\), it is clear that \(|V|\leq\prod_{j=1}^k\mathcal C(\epsilon_j,H_j,\rho_{j+1})\). Hence it remains to show that \(V\) is an \(\epsilon\)-cover for \(H\).

Suppose that \(g=g_k\circ\cdots\circ g_1\in H\). Find \(f_1,\ldots,f_k\) from the corresponding covers, and let \(f=f_k\circ\cdots\circ f_1\in V\). It suffices to show that \(d_{L_1(P;\rho_{k+1})}(f,g)\leq\epsilon\). We prove that for all \(h\), \(1\leq h\leq k\),

\[d_{L_1(P;\rho_{h+1})}(f_h\circ\cdots\circ f_1,g_h\circ\cdots\circ g_1)\leq\sum_{j=1}^h\left(\prod_{l=j+1}^hb_l\right)\epsilon_j.\]

Since \(\epsilon=\sum_{j=1}^k(\prod_{l=j+1}^kb_l)\epsilon_j\), part (1) follows. If \(h=1\) then the result follows directly from our definition of \(f_1\). Otherwise the triangle inequality, the Lipschitz assumption, the inductive hypothesis, and the definition of \(f_h\) give

\[\begin{aligned}&d_{L_1(P;\rho_{h+1})}(f_h\circ\cdots\circ f_1,g_h\circ\cdots\circ g_1)\\&\quad\leq b_h\,d_{L_1(P;\rho_h)}(f_{h-1}\circ\cdots\circ f_1,g_{h-1}\circ\cdots\circ g_1)+\epsilon_h\\&\quad\leq b_h\sum_{j=1}^{h-1}\left(\prod_{l=j+1}^{h-1}b_l\right)\epsilon_j+\epsilon_h\\&\quad=\sum_{j=1}^h\left(\prod_{l=j+1}^hb_l\right)\epsilon_j.\end{aligned}\]

To prove part (2), let \(a_j=\prod_{l=j+1}^kb_l\) and set \(\epsilon_j=\epsilon/(ka_j)\). By part (1), \(\mathcal C(\epsilon,H,\rho_{k+1})\leq\prod_{j=1}^k\mathcal C(\epsilon/(ka_j),H_j,\rho_{j+1})\). Taking \(\limsup_{\epsilon\to0}\) after logs divided by \(\log(1/\epsilon)\) gives \(\overline{\dim}(H)\leq\sum_{j=1}^k\overline{\dim}(H_j)\).

theorems.title: Capacity bound from pseudo dimension
theorems.section: 7 Sample size bounds for learning with multi-layer neural nets
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(H\) be a family of functions from \(X\) into \(A=[0,M]\). Assume \(\dim_P(H)=d\) for some \(1\leq d<\infty\).

  1. For all \(0<\epsilon\leq M\),

    \[\mathcal C(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1})<2\left(\frac{2eM}{\epsilon}\ln\frac{2eM}{\epsilon}\right)^d.\]

  2. \(\overline{\dim}(H)\leq\dim_P(H)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(P\) be any probability measure on \(X\). Then by Theorems 12 and 6 in sections 10.1 and 4,

\[\mathcal N(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1(P)})\leq\mathcal M(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1(P)})<2\left(\frac{2eM}{\epsilon}\ln\frac{2eM}{\epsilon}\right)^d.\]

(Theorem 6 is applied with \(Z=X\) and \(F=H\).) This gives (1), and (2) follows easily from (1).

theorems.title: Capacity of a feedforward architecture
theorems.section: 7 Sample size bounds for learning with multi-layer neural nets
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(\mathcal A\) be a feedforward architecture as above with \(n\geq1\) input units, \(k\geq1\) output units, and depth \(d\geq1\). Let \(W\) be the total number of adjustable weights and biases in \(\mathcal A\). Assume \(b_j\geq1\) for \(2\leq j\leq d\), and let \(H\) be all functions from \([c_0,c_1]^n\) into \([c_0,c_1]^k\) representable on \(\mathcal A\) by setting the adjustable weights and biases such that for all \(j\), \(2\leq j\leq d\), the average of the Lipschitz bounds of the functions computed by computation units at depth \(j\) is at most \(b_j\). Then for all \(0<\epsilon\leq c_2-c_1\),

\[\mathcal C(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1})\leq\left(\frac{2e(c_2-c_1)d\prod_{l=2}^db_l}{\epsilon}\right)^{2W}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
For each \(j\), \(0\leq j\leq d\), let \(n_j\) be the number of units at depth \(j\) in the architecture \(\mathcal A\). For each \(j\), \(0\leq j\leq d-1\), let \(l_j=\sum_{i=0}^jn_i\), and let \(l_d=n_d=k\). For each \(j\), \(1\leq j\leq d+1\), let \(X_j=[c_1,c_2]^{l_{j-1}}\). Then for each \(j\), \(1\leq j\leq d\), we can define the family \(H_j\) of functions from \(X_j\) into \(X_{j+1}\) in the following manner.

First assume \(j<d\). Let \(u_1,\ldots,u_{n_j}\) be an enumeration of the computation units at depth \(j\) and \(f_1,\ldots,f_{n_j}\) be functions such that \(f_i\) can be represented by \(u_i\), \(1\leq i\leq n_j\), and the average Lipschitz bound on the \(f_i\)'s is at most \(b_j\). Let \(h_j\) be the free product of \(f_1,\ldots,f_{n_j}\) and \(l_{j-1}\) copies of the identity function on \([c_1,c_2]\). Thus \(h_j:X_j\to X_{j+1}\). The function \(h_j\) represents a mapping from the sequence of all activations of units at depth at most \(j-1\) to the sequence of all activations of units at depth at most \(j\), where the activations at depth at most \(j-1\) are unaltered, and the new activations, i.e. those at depth \(j\), are calculated by \(f_1,\ldots,f_{n_j}\). The family \(H_j\) consists of all functions \(h_j\) obtained in this manner, by varying the weights and biases in the units at depth \(j\) in such a manner that the Lipschitz constraint is satisfied.

When \(j=d\), no subsequent calculations will be performed so we no longer need to preserve the activations of shallower units. Hence, we omit the identity function components in each \(h_d\in H_d\). Otherwise the definition of \(H_d\) is the same as that for \(H_j\), where \(j<d\).

It is clear that the class \(H\) in the statement of the theorem can be represented as the class of compositions of functions from classes \(H_1,\ldots,H_d\). Since the identity function has Lipschitz bound \(1\leq b_j\), the average Lipschitz bound on the components of each function \(h_j\in H_j\) is at most \(b_j\). It is easily verified that a free product function is Lipschitz bounded by the average of the Lipschitz bounds on its component functions. Hence by assumption, \(b_j\) is a uniform Lipschitz bound on \(H_j\), \(2\leq j\leq d\). For each \(j\), let \(a_j=\prod_{l=j+1}^db_l\) and \(\epsilon_j=\epsilon/(da_j)\). Since \(\epsilon<c_2-c_1\) and \(a_j\geq1\), \(\epsilon_j\leq c_2-c_1\). Let \(\rho_j\) be the \(d_{L_1}\) metric on \(X_j\). Then by Lemma 8, part (1),

\[\mathcal C(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1})\leq\prod_{j=1}^d\mathcal C(\epsilon_j,H_j,\rho_{j+1}).\]

For each \(j\), \(H_j\) is contained in the free product of \(l_j\) function classes. Each class \(F\) in this product is either the trivial class containing only the identity function, or is a finite dimensional vector space of real-valued functions, summed with a fixed modifier and then composed with a non-increasing or non-decreasing squashing function. In the latter case, the dimension \(N\) of this vector space is the number of free parameters associated with the corresponding computation unit, i.e. the number of weights plus one (for the adjustable bias). Hence by Theorems 4 and 5 in section 4, the pseudo dimension \(\dim_P(F)\leq N\). Thus by Theorem 10 above,

\[\mathcal C(\epsilon_j,F,d_{L_1})\leq2\left(\frac{2e(c_2-c_1)}{\epsilon_j}\ln\frac{2e(c_2-c_1)}{\epsilon_j}\right)^N\leq\left(\frac{2e(c_2-c_1)}{\epsilon_j}\right)^{2N},\]

since \(2\ln x<x\) and \(N\geq1\). Since the capacity of a class with only one function is 1, it follows from Lemma 7 part (1) that

\[\mathcal C(\epsilon_j,H_j,\rho_{j+1})\leq\left(\frac{2e(c_2-c_1)}{\epsilon_j}\right)^{2W_j},\]

where \(W_j\) is the total number of weights and biases of all computation nodes at depth \(j\). Multiplying these bounds over all \(j\), it follows that

\[\begin{aligned}\mathcal C(\epsilon,H,d_{L_1})&\leq\prod_{j=1}^d\left(\frac{2e(c_2-c_1)}{\epsilon_j}\right)^{2W_j}\\&=\prod_{j=1}^d\left(\frac{2e(c_2-c_1)d\prod_{l=j+1}^db_l}{\epsilon}\right)^{2W_j}\\&\leq\left(\frac{2e(c_2-c_1)d\prod_{l=2}^db_l}{\epsilon}\right)^{2W}.\end{aligned}\]

theorems.title: Sample bounds for multilayer neural networks
theorems.section: 7 Sample size bounds for learning with multi-layer neural nets
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(n,k,H,W,d\) and \(b_2,\ldots,b_d\) be as in the previous theorem. Let \(X\) be the instance space \([c_1,c_2]^n\), \(A\) be the decision space \([c_1,c_2]^k\), and \(Y\) be any outcome space. Let \(l:Y imes A o[0,M]\) be a loss function and \(c_l\) be a constant such that \( ho_l(\vec a,\vec b)\leq c_l d_{L_1}(\vec a,\vec b)\) for all \(\vec a,\vec b\in A\). Let \(m\geq1\), \(0< u\leq8(c_2-c_1)\), \(0<\alpha<1\), and \(P\) be any probability distribution on \(Z=X imes Y\). Let \(\vec z\) be generated by \(m\) independent random draws from \(Z\) according to \(P\).

  1. Then

    \[\Pr igl(\exists f\in H:d_ u(\widehat r_{f;l}(\vec z),r_{f;l}(P))>\alpha igr)\leq4\left( rac{c_l16e(c_2-c_1)d\prod_{l=2}^db_l}{\alpha u} ight)^{2W}e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}.\]

  2. Assume that for each computation unit at depth 2 and above the number of weights is at most \(W_{\max}\), no weight is allowed to have absolute value greater than \( eta\), the input transformers are identity functions, the global modifier has Lipschitz bound at most \(r\), and the squashing function has Lipschitz bound at most \(s\), where \(s( eta W_{\max}+r)\geq1\). Then for any \(0<\delta<1\), the probability in (1) is less than \(\delta\) for sample size

    \[m=O\!\left( rac{M}{\alpha^2 u}\left(W\left(\log rac{c_l(c_2-c_1)}{\alpha u}+d\log(s( eta W_{\max}+r)) ight)+\log rac1\delta ight) ight).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(\epsilon=\alpha u/8\leq c_2-c_1\). It can be verified that \(l_H\) is permissible for the decision rule space \(H\). Hence, using Theorem 9 and Theorem 11,

\[ egin{aligned}\Pr(\exists f\in H:d_ u(\widehat r_{f;l}(\vec z),r_{f;l}(P))>\alpha)&\leq4\mathcal C(\alpha u/8,H, ho_l)e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}\&\leq4\mathcal C(\alpha u/(8c_l),H,d_{L_1})e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}\&\leq4\left( rac{c_l16e(c_2-c_1)d\prod_{l=2}^db_l}{\alpha u} ight)^{2W}e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}.\end{aligned}\]

For the second bound, it is readily verified that the \(L_1\) Lipschitz bound for a linear function defined by \(W_{\max}\) weights and a bias is no more than \(W_{\max}\) times the largest absolute value of any weight. Furthermore, the Lipschitz bound for the sum of two functions is no more than the sum of the Lipschitz bounds on the individual functions, and the Lipschitz bound for the composition of two functions is no more than the product of the Lipschitz bounds on the individual functions. Thus if the input transformers are identity functions, the global modifier and squashing function have Lipschitz bounds \(r\) and \(s\) respectively and no weight is allowed to have absolute value greater than \( eta\), then the Lipschitz bound for a computation unit is at most \(s( eta W_{\max}+r)\). If this holds for all units at depth 2 and above, we may take \(b_j=s( eta W_{\max}+r)\) for all \(j\geq2\) in the first bound. Solving for \(m\), this gives the order-of-magnitude estimate of the second bound.

theorems.title: Packing and covering inequalities
theorems.section: 10.1 Metric spaces, covering numbers and metric dimension
theorems.proofNote: The paper introduces these inequalities as easily verified and gives no separate proof.
theorems.statementHtml:
If \(T\) is a totally bounded subset of the (pseudo) metric space \((S,\rho)\) then for any \(\epsilon>0\),

\[\mathcal M(2\epsilon,T,\rho)\leq\mathcal N(\epsilon,T,\rho)\leq\mathcal M(\epsilon,T,\rho).\]

theorems.title: Deviation bounds in the d-nu metric
theorems.section: 10.3 Measuring the accuracy of empirical estimates with the dν metric
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(Z_1,\ldots,Z_n\) be i.i.d. random variables with range \(0\leq Z_i\leq M\) and \(E(Z_i)=\mu\), \(1\leq i\leq n\). Assume \( u>0\) and \(0<\alpha<1\). Then

  1. \[\Pr\left(d_ u\left( rac1n\sum_{i=1}^nZ_i,\mu ight)>\alpha ight)\leq rac{M^2}{4\alpha^2 u n(M+ u)}< rac{M}{4\alpha^2 u n}.\]
  2. \[\Pr\left(d_ u\left( rac1n\sum_{i=1}^nZ_i,\mu ight)>\alpha ight)\leq2e^{-18\alpha^2 u n/((3+\alpha)^2M)}<2e^{-9\alpha^2 u n/(8M)}<2e^{-\alpha^2 u n/M}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Let \(Y_i=Z_i-\mu\), \(1\leq i\leq n\). Then

\[ egin{aligned}\Pr\left(d_ u\left( rac1n\sum_{i=1}^nZ_i,\mu ight)>\alpha ight)&=\Pr\left( rac{|(\sum_{i=1}^nZ_i)-\mu n|}{ u n+\sum_{i=1}^nZ_i+\mu n}>\alpha ight)\&\leq\Pr\left(\left|\sum_{i=1}^nY_i ight|>\alpha n( u+\mu) ight),\end{aligned}\]

since \(\sum_{i=1}^nZ_i\geq0\).

To obtain the first bound, note that by Chebyshev's inequality,

\[\Pr\left(\left|\sum_{i=1}^nY_i ight|>\alpha n( u+\mu) ight)\leq\sigma^2/(\alpha n( u+\mu))^2,\]

where \(\sigma^2=n\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)\). Since \(0\leq Z_i\leq M\), \(\operatorname{Var}(Z_i)=\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)\leq\mu(M-\mu)\). Hence

\[\Pr\left(\left|\sum_{i=1}^nY_i ight|>\alpha n( u+\mu) ight)\leq rac{\mu(M-\mu)}{\alpha^2n( u+\mu)^2}.\]

It is easily verified that the maximum value of this expression occurs at \(\mu=( u M)/(2 u+M)\), and that this gives an upper bound of \(M^2/(4\alpha^2 u n(M+ u))\).

To obtain the second bound, we apply Bernstein's inequality (see e.g. [Pol84], Page 192), which states that

\[\Pr\left(\left|\sum_{i=1}^nY_i ight|>\eta ight)\leq2e^{-\eta^2/(2(n\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)+B\eta/3))}\]

for any zero mean i.i.d. random variables \(Y_1,\ldots,Y_n\) bounded in absolute value by \(B\). Substituting \(\eta=\alpha n( u+\mu)\) and upper bounds \(B\leq M\) and \(\operatorname{Var}(Y_i)\leq\mu M\), this gives a bound of

\[2e^{-n\alpha^2( u+\mu)^2/(2\mu M+2M\alpha( u+\mu)/3)}=2e^{- rac{3n\alpha^2}{2M} rac{( u+\mu)^2}{\alpha u+(3+\alpha)\mu}}.\]

Since \(( u+\mu)^2/(\alpha u+(3+\alpha)\mu)\) is minimized at \(\mu=(3-\alpha) u/(3+\alpha)\), the latter expression is bounded by substituting this value of \(\mu\). This gives the first bound of part (2). The second bound follows from the fact that \(\alpha<1\).

theorems.title: A property of the extended metric
theorems.section: 10.4 Proof of Main Theorem on Uniform Convergence of Empirical Estimates
theorems.statementHtml:
For all \(\vec x,\vec y\in(\mathbb R^+)^{2m}\) and \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\),

\[d(\mu_1(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec x,\sigma))\leq d(\mu_1(\vec y,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec y,\sigma))+\widetilde d(\vec x,\vec y).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
We have

\[\begin{aligned}d(\mu_1(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec x,\sigma))&\leq d(\mu_1(\vec y,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec y,\sigma))\\&\quad+d(\mu_1(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_1(\vec y,\sigma))+d(\mu_2(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec y,\sigma))\end{aligned}\]

by the triangle inequality on \(d\). The last two terms of this sum combined are at most \(\widetilde d(\vec x,\vec y)\) by definition.

theorems.title: Random-swap exponential inequality
theorems.section: 10.4 Proof of Main Theorem on Uniform Convergence of Empirical Estimates
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(\vec x=(x_1,\ldots,x_{2m})\) be a sequence of reals such that \(0\leq x_i\leq M\), \(1\leq i\leq2m\). Assume \( u>0\) and \(0<\alpha<1\). Then if a permutation \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\) is chosen uniformly at random,

\[\Pr igl(d_ u(\mu_1(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec x,\sigma))>\alpha igr)\leq2e^{-2\alpha^2 u m/M}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
For each \(i\), \(1\leq i\leq m\), let \(Y_i\) be an independent random variable such that \(Y_i=x_i-x_{m+i}\) with probability \(1/2\) and \(Y_i=x_{m+i}-x_i\) with probability \(1/2\). Note that for any \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\),

\[d_ u(\mu_1(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec x,\sigma))= rac{|\sum_{i=1}^m(x_{\sigma(i)}-x_{\sigma(m+i)})|}{ u m+\sum_{i=1}^{2m}x_i}.\]

Hence

\[\Pr igl(d_ u(\mu_1(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec x,\sigma))>\alpha igr)=\Pr\left(\left|\sum_{i=1}^mY_i ight|>\alpha\left( u m+\sum_{i=1}^{2m}x_i ight) ight),\]

because each swap in a randomly chosen \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\) is independent. Since \(E(Y_i)=0\) and \(-|x_i-x_{m+i}|\leq Y_i\leq|x_i-x_{m+i}|\), we can apply Hoeffding's inequality to bound the latter probability by

\[2e^{-2\alpha^2( u m+\sum_{i=1}^{2m}x_i)^2/(2\sum_{i=1}^m(x_i-x_{m+i})^2)}.\]

Let \( eta=\sum_{i=1}^{2m}x_i\). Since \(0\leq x_i\leq M\),

\[\sum_{i=1}^m(x_i-x_{m+i})^2\leq\sum_{i=1}^mM|x_i-x_{m+i}|\leq eta M.\]

Hence we have

\[2e^{-2\alpha^2( u m+ eta)^2/(2 eta M)}=2e^{- rac{\alpha^2}{M} rac{( u m+ eta)^2}{ eta}}.\]

The expression in parentheses is minimized, and therefore the whole expression is maximized, by setting \( eta= u m\), giving a value of \(4 u m\). Hence

\[\Pr igl(d_ u(\mu_1(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec x,\sigma))>\alpha igr)\leq2e^{-2\alpha^2 u m/M}.\]

theorems.title: Symmetrization in the d-nu metric
theorems.section: 10.4 Proof of Main Theorem on Uniform Convergence of Empirical Estimates
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be a permissible set of functions on \(Z\) with \(0\leq f(z)\leq M\) for all \(f\in F\) and \(z\in Z\). Assume \( u>0\), \(0<\alpha<1\) and \(m\geq2M/(\alpha^2 u)\). Then

\[\Pr\{\vec z\in Z^m:\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha\}\leq2\Pr\{\vec z\in Z^{2m}:\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E'_{\vec z}(f),\widehat E''_{\vec z}(f))>\alpha/2\}.\]
theorems.proofHtml:
If \(Z\) and \(F\) are uncountable, the assumption of permissibility guarantees that these probabilities are well-defined (see section 10.2 and [Pol84]). From Chebyshev's inequality (see Lemma 9, part (1) in section 10.3), for each individual \(f\in F\),

\[\Pr\{\vec z\in Z^m:d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha/2\}\leq M/(\alpha^2 u m).\]

Since \(m\geq2M/(\alpha^2 u)\), this probability is at most \(1/2\). Now consider any \(f\in F\) and sample \(\vec z'\in Z^m\) such that \(d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z'}(f),E(f))>\alpha\). If we draw an independent random sample \(\vec z''\in Z^m\), then with probability at least \(1/2\), \(d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z''}(f),E(f))\leq\alpha/2\). Whenever this happens we have \(d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z'}(f),\widehat E_{\vec z''}(f))>\alpha/2\) by the triangle inequality for \(d_ u\). Thus

\[ egin{aligned}&\Pr\{\vec z\in Z^{2m}:\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E'_{\vec z}(f),\widehat E''_{\vec z}(f))>\alpha/2\}\&\quad\geq\Pr\{\vec z'\vec z''\in Z^{2m}:\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z'}(f),E(f))>\alpha ext{ and }d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z''}(f),E(f))\leq\alpha/2\}\&\quad\geq rac12\Pr\{\vec z'\in Z^m:\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z'}(f),E(f))>\alpha\}.\end{aligned}\]

Again, when \(Z\) and \(F\) are uncountable, permissibility guarantees that the implied use of Fubini's theorem in obtaining the above inequalities is justified.

theorems.title: Uniform convergence using the extended metric
theorems.section: 10.4 Proof of Main Theorem on Uniform Convergence of Empirical Estimates
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(F\) be a permissible set of functions on \(Z\) with \(0\leq f(z)\leq M\) for all \(f\in F\) and \(z\in Z\). Assume \( u>0\), \(0<\alpha<1\) and \(m\geq1\). Let

\[p(\alpha, u,m)=\Pr\{\vec z\in Z^m:\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(f),E(f))>\alpha\}.\]

Then

\[p(\alpha, u,m)\leq2E\left(\min\left(2\mathcal N(\alpha/4,F|_{\vec z};\widetilde d_ u)e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)},1 ight) ight),\]

and if in addition \(F|_{\vec z}\) is finite for all \(\vec z\in Z^{2m}\) then

\[p(\alpha, u,m)\leq2E\left(\min\left(2|F|_{\vec z}|e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(2M)},1 ight) ight),\]

where the expectations are over \(\vec z\) drawn randomly from \(Z^{2m}\).
theorems.proofHtml:
First note that both bounds are trivial if \(m<2M/(\alpha^2 u)\), so we may assume \(m\geq2M/(\alpha^2 u)\). Hence by Lemma 12,

\[p(\alpha, u,m)\leq2\Pr\{\vec z\in Z^{2m}:\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E'_{\vec z}(f),\widehat E''_{\vec z}(f))>\alpha/2\}.\]

Thus it suffices to obtain bounds for the latter quantity.

We begin with the second bound, for the case when \(F|_{\vec z}\) is always finite. For any sample \(\vec z=(z_1,\ldots,z_{2m})\in Z^{2m}\) and \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\), let \(\sigma(\vec z)=(z_{\sigma(1)},\ldots,z_{\sigma(2m)})\). For any fixed function \(f\in F\) and fixed \(\vec z\in Z^{2m}\), if we select a permutation \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\) uniformly at random,

\[\Pr igl(d_ u(\widehat E'_{\sigma(\vec z)}(f),\widehat E''_{\sigma(\vec z)}(f))>\alpha/2 igr)\leq2e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(2M)}. ag{7}\]

by Lemma 11. Hence for any fixed \(\vec z\in Z^{2m}\), if we select \(\sigma\) uniformly at random,

\[\Pr igl(\exists f\in F ext{ with }d_ u(\widehat E'_{\sigma(\vec z)}(f),\widehat E''_{\sigma(\vec z)}(f))>\alpha/2 igr)\leq\min(2|F|_{\vec z}|e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(2M)},1). ag{8}\]

Thus if we draw \(\vec z\) at random from \(Z^{2m}\) and independently select a permutation \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\) uniformly at random, the same probability is at most \(E(\min(2|F|_{\vec z}|e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(2M)},1))\). However, since each of the \(2m\) observations in \(\vec z\) are independent, each of the samples \(\sigma(\vec z)\) is equally likely. Hence this is the same probability obtained when only the sample \(\vec z\) is chosen at random. The second bound follows.

The proof of the first bound is similar, except for steps 7 and 8. From Lemma 10, using the extension \(\widetilde d_ u\), if \(f,f^*\in F\) are such that

\[\widetilde d_ u((f(z_1),\ldots,f(z_{2m})),(f^*(z_1),\ldots,f^*(z_{2m})))\leq\alpha/4,\]

then for any \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\) such that \(d_ u(\widehat E'_{\sigma(\vec z)}(f),\widehat E''_{\sigma(\vec z)}(f))>\alpha/2\), we must have \(d_ u(\widehat E'_{\sigma(\vec z)}(f^*),\widehat E''_{\sigma(\vec z)}(f^*))>\alpha/4\). Thus if \(N\) is an \(\alpha/4\)-cover for \(F|_{\vec z}\) with respect to \(\widetilde d_ u\), whenever there exists such an \(f\), there exists \(f^*\in N\) with the latter inequality. For fixed \(f^*\) and random \(\sigma\), its probability is at most \(2e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)}\) by Lemma 11. Hence for any fixed \(\vec z\), the required probability is at most

\[\min(2\mathcal N(\alpha/4,F|_{\vec z};\widetilde d_ u)e^{-\alpha^2 u m/(8M)},1).\]

The remainder of the proof is as above.

theorems.title: Extended metric controlled by L1
theorems.section: 10.4 Proof of Main Theorem on Uniform Convergence of Empirical Estimates
theorems.statementHtml: For any \(m\geq1\), \(\vec x,\vec y\in(\mathbb R^+)^{2m}\) and \( u>0\), \(\widetilde d_ u(\vec x,\vec y)\leq(2/ u)d_{L_1}(\vec x,\vec y)\).
theorems.proofHtml:
For any \(\sigma\in\Gamma_{2m}\),

\[ egin{aligned}&d_ u(\mu_1(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_1(\vec y,\sigma))+d_ u(\mu_2(\vec x,\sigma),\mu_2(\vec y,\sigma))\&\quad= rac{|\sum_{i=1}^m(x_{\sigma(i)}-y_{\sigma(i)})|}{ u m+\sum_{i=1}^m(x_{\sigma(i)}+y_{\sigma(i)})}+ rac{|\sum_{i=m+1}^{2m}(x_{\sigma(i)}-y_{\sigma(i)})|}{ u m+\sum_{i=m+1}^{2m}(x_{\sigma(i)}+y_{\sigma(i)})}\&\quad\leq rac{\sum_{i=1}^{2m}|x_{\sigma(i)}-y_{\sigma(i)}|}{ u m}= rac2 u d_{L_1}(\vec x,\vec y).\end{aligned}\]

The result follows.

theorems.title: Sharp-threshold neural-network uniform convergence
theorems.section: 10.5 Bounds on sample size for learning in feedforward nets with sharp thresholds
theorems.statementHtml:
Let \(\mathcal A\) be a feedforward architecture as defined in section 7 with \(n\geq1\) inputs, one output, \(N\geq2\) computation units and a total of \(W\) weights and biases. Let \(X=\mathbb R^n\), \(Y=A=\{0,1\}\), and \(l\) be the discrete loss function. Assume that the squashing function for each computation unit has the form \(\operatorname{sign}\circ\sigma\), where \(\sigma\) is non-decreasing or non-increasing. Different \(\sigma\)'s can be used for different units. Let \(H\) be all functions from \(X\) into \(A\) representable on \(\mathcal A\) by varying the weights and biases. Let \( u>0\), \(0<\alpha<1\) and \(m\geq1\). Suppose that \(\vec z\) is generated by \(m\) independent random draws from a probability measure \(P\) on \(Z=X imes Y\). Then

\[\Pr igl(\exists h\in H:d_ u(\widehat r_{h;l}(\vec z),r_{h;l}(P))>\alpha igr)\leq4(2eNm/W)^W e^{-\alpha^2 u m/2}.\]

This probability is at most \(\delta\) for a sample size \(m\) that is

\[O\!\left( rac1{\alpha^2 u}\left(W\log rac N{\alpha u}+\log rac1\delta ight) ight).\]
theorems.proofHtml:
Each computation unit in the network with \(k\) weights is associated with a class of \(\{0,1\}\)-valued functions of the form

\[f(\vec x)=\operatorname{sign}\left(\sigma(\phi_1(\vec x),\ldots,\phi_k(\vec x))+ heta+\sum_{j=1}^kw_j\phi_j(\vec x) ight)\]

where \( heta,w_1,\ldots,w_k\) are adjustable real-valued parameters and \(\phi_1,\ldots,\phi_k,\mu\) and \(\sigma\) are fixed functions, the latter monotone. By Theorems 4 and 5 this class of functions has pseudo dimension at most \(k+1\). Since the pseudo dimension is the same as the Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension for classes of indicator functions, this implies that the class has Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimension at most \(k+1\). Now let \(d\) be the sum of all the Vapnik–Chervonenkis dimensions of all the classes of functions associated with the computation units of the architecture \(\mathcal A\). It follows that \(d\leq W\), the total number of weights and biases in the network.

For each \(h\in H\) let \(l_h\) be the loss function associated with \(h\) for the discrete loss \(l\), i.e. \(l_h(x,y)=1\) if \(y eq h(x)\), \(l_h(x,y)=0\) if \(y=h(x)\). Let \(F=l_H=\{l_h:h\in H\}\). Let \(\vec z=((x_1,y_1),\ldots,(x_m,y_m))\) be any fixed sample and \(\vec x=(x_1,\ldots,x_m)\). It is easily verified that \(|F|_{\vec z}|=|H|_{\vec x}|\). It is shown in [BH89], Theorem 1, (and is also implied directly from results in [Cov68]) that for any class \(H\) as above, \(|H|_{\vec x}|\leq(Nem/d)^d\) for all \(\vec x\), where \(d\) and \(N\) are as above. This implies that

\[|F|_{\vec z}|\leq(Nem/d)^d\leq(Nem/W)^W\]

for all samples \(\vec z\) of length \(m\). Since each \(l_h\in F\) is a random variable that is bounded between 0 and 1, Theorem 3 (second part) shows that

\[\Pr igl(\exists h\in F:d_ u(\widehat E_{\vec z}(h),E(h))>\alpha igr)\leq4(2eNm/W)^W e^{-\alpha^2 u m/2}.\]

This gives the first bound.

For the second bound, it can be shown that for sample size

\[m= rac4{\alpha^2 u}\left(2W\ln rac{16N}{\alpha^2 u}+\ln rac4\delta ight)\]

we have \(4(2eNm/W)^We^{-\alpha^2 u m/2}\leq\delta\). To see this, first note that by rearranging, we get

\[\alpha^2 u m/2\geq W\ln(2eNm/W)+\ln(4/\delta),\]

thus it suffices to show \(\alpha^2 u m/4\geq W\ln(2eNm/W)\) and \(\alpha^2 u m/4\geq\ln(4/\delta)\). The latter inequality is assured by the last term in the formula for \(m\). For the former inequality, let us take \(m\) equal to the first term only, i.e. \(m=8W(\alpha^2 u)^{-1}\ln(16N/(\alpha^2 u))\). Substituting this into the former inequality and simplifying, we get

\[2\ln rac{16N}{\alpha^2 u}\geq\ln\left( rac{16eN}{\alpha^2 u}\ln rac{16N}{\alpha^2 u} ight).\]

This further simplifies to

\[ rac{16N}{\alpha^2 u}\geq e\ln rac{16N}{\alpha^2 u},\]

which holds, since \(x\geq e\ln x\) for any \(x\). Finally, since this inequality holds for the given \(m\), it is easy to see that it will also hold for larger \(m\).
